title: Chapter 20: The Drop
content: The USB drive sat between them on the Ikea table like a grenade with the pin half-pulled.

Chloe had stripped the plastic casing off one end, soldered a diagnostic lead to the NAND chip, and spent four hours verifying every byte against the SHA-256 hash Kapoor had scribbled on a napkin. 4.7 gigabytes. The entire anatomy of a horror show. Now she leaned back in her chair, surrounded by a graveyard of takeout containers and the ammoniac tang of burnt coffee, and told Maya what she'd built.

"Fourteen servers. Nine countries. Estonia, Iceland, Brazil, South Africa — jurisdictions that don't hand over data without a fight." Chloe's fingers danced across her keyboard, and a world map bloomed across the screen, fourteen pinpricks of green light scattered like constellations. "The evidence is split, encrypted with separate AES-256 keys. No single server has the full picture. Without all fourteen shards and the reassembly script, the data is noise."
" heartbeat?"

"Forty-eight hours." Chloe tapped a terminal window where a countdown pulsed in the corner — 47:58:12, 47:58:11. "Every check-in window, you, me, Jordi, and Kapoor each have six hours to ping the system. Any method — encrypted email, Signal, even a specific Wikipedia edit. If all four of us miss our windows, the switch triggers. The shards reassemble, decrypt, and auto-publish to every major news outlet, every social media platform, every true crime subreddit with more than ten thousand members." She turned from the screen. Her eyes were bloodshot, iron-veined. She hadn't slept. "It's a nuclear option."

Maya picked up the USB drive. The bare chip was warm from the soldering iron, smooth and sharp at the edges where the plastic had been stripped. She felt the weight of it — barely anything, a few grams — and thought about the bodies that weight represented.
"If we go down," Chloe said, "the truth goes everywhere. Vlad can't kill us all and bury the data. That's the point. Mutually assured destruction."
"What about Kapoor?"

"His check-in is the first one. Six hours from now. If he doesn't ping, the grace period starts. After forty-eight hours with no contact from any of us — boom." Chloe closed her laptop gently, like settling a sleeping child. "He knows the stakes. He gave us the drive."
Maya set the USB on the table. The morning light through the Airbnb's thin curtains turned it into a small black obelisk. "Then we're committed."
"We were committed the second we took that drive."
Jordi called at eleven. His voice had the compressed, reedy quality of someone speaking from inside a car, inside a parking garage, inside a spiral of bad news.
"They killed it. Again."
Maya closed her eyes. "Legal?"

"Legal, advertiser pressure, and — get this — 'unverifiable supernatural claims.'" She could hear him hitting the steering wheel. A dull thud. "I've got twelve thousand words, Maya. Meticulously sourced. I've got the Kapoor forensics, the blood type catalogs cross-referenced with Lumina user data, the Dutch East India Company shipping manifests from 1647 that match Aeterna's own internal genealogy charts. I had a First Amendment attorney review it line by line. And my editor — my editor who ran the Theranos takedown, who ran the Facebook Files — looked at all of it and said, 'We can't verify the supernatural elements to publication standard.'"
"Because there's no peer-reviewed study on vampires."
"Because there's no peer-reviewed study on vampires." He laughed, but it was the kind of laugh that strips paint. "So I'm publishing it myself. On Substack. Tomorrow at noon."

Silence settled between them. Maya could hear the garage's fluorescent lights buzzing through the phone — that cheap, mercury-vapor hum that made every parking structure feel like a crime scene waiting to happen.
"Are you sure?" she asked. "Once it's out there, there's no going back."

"There was never any going back. Not for me." His voice dropped. "You know that. You were at that dinner. You heard what Vlad offered. There's no version of this where we walk away clean and go back to our lives. There's just the version where we tell the truth and the version where we don't."

Maya pressed her palm flat against the Airbnb's kitchen counter. The laminate was cool and slightly sticky — someone else's spilled something, never wiped it properly. "Tomorrow at noon. I'll have the podcast ready to drop at the same time."
"Two bombs. Same detonation."
"He won't know which way to run."

She recorded Episode 3 at 2 a.m., hunched over the borrowed microphone in the Airbnb's closet because it was the only space with enough acoustic deadening. The closet smelled like cedar and old coats and the vinegar note of her own fear-sweat, but the audio came out clean.
This one was different.
The first two episodes had been careful. Hints. Speculation. The kind of connect-the-dots that let listeners feel smart for drawing their own conclusions. Episode 3 was a direct hit.
"This is Maya Reyes, and this is Unfollowed. Episode three. And this time, I'm going to tell you exactly what's happening, and I'm going to name the people doing it."
She said the word: Aeterna. She said the name: Alexander Vlad. She said the app: Lumina. She said the place: Sublevel B.
Then she played the evidence.

She played the blood type catalogs — voice-reading the spreadsheet columns because the raw documents would need forensic authentication, but the data spoke for itself. Type A-positive, donor ID 2847, harvest date, specimen viability score. She read the metrics aloud, her voice flat and clinical, letting the words do the mutilation themselves. Specimen viability. As if a person were a tissue sample. As if a body were a crop.

She played the Dutch-language records. Aeterna's own internal archives — or rather, the archives of the organizations that preceded Aeterna, stretching back to the 1600s, written in a cramped merchant's hand that documented shipping routes and cargo manifests for the Dutch East India Company. But the cargo wasn't spices. The cargo was typed in a different column. Donor acquisitions. Longevity yields. The same terminology, three and a half centuries apart.
She played the sound of herself breathing — couldn't edit it out, didn't want to. Human breath against the machinery of centuries.
Then she addressed him directly.

"Alexander Vlad." Her voice was steady. The closet was small and dark and her knees were drawn up to her chest and she was not steady at all, but her voice was. "You offered me my friend's life in exchange for my silence. You told me Lucy Chen was alive. You dangled her like bait. Here is my answer."
She reached out. Her finger found the trackpad. The cursor hovered over the button. The click was small and definite — a plastic sound, a mechanical sound, the sound of a pin leaving a grenade.
The podcast published.
**FORMAT BREAK: THE DROP**

**6:02 PM PST** — #BloodHarvest begins trending on Twitter. Within twenty minutes it jumps from the technology sidebar to the worldwide trending list. The hashtag sits between a soccer match and a K-pop announcement, obscene in its normalcy.

**6:14 PM** — A TikTok user named @truecrimedumpster posts a 60-second breakdown of Episode 3. Her voice is squeaky with adrenaline. "Y'ALL. I've been listening to this podcast since episode one and I thought it was ARG marketing BUT IT'S REAL." The video gets 400,000 views in its first hour.

**6:31 PM** — r/UnresolvedMysteries pins a megathread: "Aeterna Biotechnologies / 'The Blood Harvest' — evidence discussion." Within two hours it has 14,000 comments. A user with a Dutch username posts a partial translation of the 1647 shipping manifest. Another user — a hematologist — analyzes the blood type catalog and notes the overrepresentation of HLA-B27 negatives, a subtype associated with autoimmune resistance. "This isn't random sampling," they write. "This is selective harvesting."

**7:00 PM** — Women begin posting screenshots. Lumina app dashboards. The fertility tracking features. The blood work integration. The location permissions they'd accepted without reading. Post after post after post: "I used this app. I used this app. I used this app."

**7:45 PM** — Congressman David Tran (CA-17) tweets: "The allegations against Aeterna Biotechnologies are deeply disturbing. I am calling for an immediate congressional investigation. Silicon Valley cannot police itself."

**9:03 PM** — Maya sits on the Airbnb floor with her phone in both hands and watches the number climb. Spotify plays. Apple Podcasts plays. YouTube views. The metrics refresh like a slot machine hitting jackpot over and over.
One million plays in six hours.
The dam had broken.

She could hear it — not literally, but she could feel it, the way you feel a bassline through the floor of a club. The internet was roaring. A million voices raised at once, amplifying, signal-boosting, picking apart the evidence with a ferocity no newsroom could match. Somewhere in that noise were people who would verify what she couldn't. Somewhere in that noise were the next two million listeners.
Maya put the phone down. Her hands were shaking. Not from fear. From the sheer kinetic release of it — the truth, finally, unchained.
The stock ticker told a different story.

She pulled up Aeterna's after-hours trading on her laptop. The green line that had been climbing steadily for months — the line that represented billions of dollars of confidence, of institutional faith, of the market's absolute certainty that Aeterna Biotechnologies was the future — that line stuttered and dropped.
8% in three hours.

She watched the number hemorrhage. She imagined the algorithmic trading bots freaking out in their silent, millisecond way. She imagined portfolio managers in Greenwich and Midtown hitting refresh on their terminals and reaching for their antacids. She imagined Vlad, somewhere, watching the same line.
Then the statement came through.

Whitby Communications — the crisis PR firm you hired when the blood was in the water and you needed it back in the veins — issued a press release at 10:17 PM. Maya read it on her phone, the blue screen-light making her face feel cold.

*"The allegations against Aeterna are categorically false. The so-called evidence is fabricated and derived from stolen proprietary data. Aeterna's groundbreaking work in longevity medicine is the product of decades of legitimate scientific research, conducted in full compliance with all regulatory standards. We are pursuing all available legal remedies against the parties responsible for this malicious disinformation campaign."*
Categorically false. Fabricated. Stolen proprietary data. Malicious disinformation.
They were doubling down.
And then Maya watched something that made her stomach fold in on itself: the stock ticked upward. 1%. 2%. 3%.
The market believed wealth. Not truth.

Three percent recovered on a denial. Three percent, because Whitby Communications was expensive and good at their job, because Aeterna's institutional investors wanted to believe the line about malicious disinformation, because the market's infinite wisdom had decided that a press release from a billion-dollar company was more credible than a million people listening to a podcast in their cars and their kitchens and their beds.

Maya set the phone on the counter. She stared at the takeout containers — Thai food from yesterday, the pad thai congealed into a pinkish brick, the smell of fish sauce and stale peanuts mixing with the burnt coffee still sitting in the pot. She thought: This could work. They could bury us. They have the money. They have the lawyers. They have the infrastructure of institutional credibility. We have a podcast and a Substack and a dead man's switch.
She thought: We might lose this.
Then Chloe's monitoring system chirped.
Not a chirp. More like a single bright tone — the sound Chloe had programmed for priority-one alerts. Maya had never heard it before. It cut through the stale-aired room like a blade.
Chloe was at her workstation in seconds, fingers already moving. "What —"
"Lucy's Apple Watch." Chloe's voice was tight, controlled, the voice of someone reading an EKG during cardiac arrest. "The one we confiscated from Aeterna. It just came back online."
Maya was behind her, looking over her shoulder at the screen. The monitoring dashboard — a black field with signal traces and device logs — showed a single spike. Green. Brief. Defiant.
"Eleven seconds," Chloe said. "It was online for exactly eleven seconds." Her fingers flew. Terminal windows opened and closed. "It transmitted one data burst. One."
"A data burst of what?"

"A GPS coordinate." Chloe pulled up a map. The view zoomed — country, state, county, satellite. The Santa Cruz mountains swelled up on screen, thickly forested, roadless in patches. The map centered on a single point: a red pin, dropped in the middle of private acreage.
"Not Aeterna HQ," Maya said.

"Not the Palo Alto campus." Chloe was already running property records. The search results populated: 47 acres, private estate, wooded, remote. Owned by a shell company registered in the Cayman Islands. Layered ownership — she peeled back two more shells before the trail dissolved into attorney-client obfuscation. "Whoever owns this went to a lot of trouble to stay invisible."

Maya leaned closer. The screen's light painted her face in satellite-map green and gray. The GPS pin sat in a clearing — she could see it now, a structure half-hidden under redwood canopy. A building. Maybe several. The kind of place you'd choose if you wanted to be alone with something you never wanted found.
"That's not Sublevel B," Maya said quietly. "That's... somewhere else."

"Lucy got her watch back." Chloe's voice was careful. "Or she found a way to transmit from a new location. Either way, she got that signal out in eleven seconds. That's not an accident. That's deliberate."
"Eleven seconds is not a lot of time to transmit a precise GPS location and then go dark again."

"No. It's not. It implies she had limited access. A window. Maybe she's been waiting for one." Chloe turned to face Maya. "Or it implies someone wanted us to see that location. Someone who knew we were monitoring the watch's MAC address."

The room was very quiet. The only sound was the hum of Chloe's laptop fan and the distant, barely audible white noise of the highway — a sound Maya had stopped noticing days ago but now heard with sudden, sharp clarity.
A trap. Or a message. The same thing, from a vampire.

Maya looked back at the screen. The red pin sat on the map like a drop of blood on green linen. Santa Cruz mountains. 47 acres. Shell company. Cayman Islands. A place with no name and no face and no reason to exist except to be invisible.
Lucy's first direct signal in days. The first proof, however ambiguous, that she was alive and thinking and fighting.
Hope.
Or hazard.
Maya stared at the pin, and the pin stared back.
